chunks: Sigiriya, known as Lion Rock, is a 5th-century rock fortress and palace ruin located in the Matale District of Sri Lanka. It rises 200 metres above the surrounding plains and is one of the most remarkable examples of ancient engineering and architecture.

King Kassapa I built Sigiriya between 477 and 495 AD. He chose this impregnable granite rock as his capital after seizing the throne by entombing his father King Dhatusena. Kassapa feared retaliation from his brother Moggallana.

After King Kassapa defeated his father and took the throne, he constructed his entire palace on the summit of the 200-metre granite rock. He surrounded it with gardens, water features, and a massive fortified outer wall.

The Lion Gate is the monumental entrance to the summit staircase. Two massive lion paws carved in brick and plaster flank the entrance. Originally a complete colossal lion head rose above the paws, but only the feet survive today. Visitors walk between the lion's claws to climb to the palace.

The Mirror Wall at Sigiriya is a polished plaster wall that runs along the western face of the rock. It was so finely polished that King Kassapa could see his reflection. Over the centuries, visitors from the 8th to 10th centuries inscribed over 685 graffiti poems and verses on its surface.

The Sigiriya Frescoes are vivid paintings of celestial maidens known as Apsaras. They are sheltered in a natural pocket halfway up the rock face. Of an original 500 paintings, 22 survive today. They depict women from the waist up holding lotus flowers, painted using lime plaster and natural pigments.

The Water Gardens at Sigiriya are considered the oldest landscaped gardens in Asia, dating back approximately 1,500 years. They feature symmetrical geometrical paths, water channels, fountains, and pools. The hydraulic system uses underground pipes and gravity to feed the fountains, which still operate naturally during the rainy season.

The Summit Palace occupies the entire 1.6-hectare plateau at the top of Sigiriya. Remains include brick walls, a throne platform, cisterns cut into the rock, and a royal pool. The summit offers a panoramic view across the jungle plains of central Sri Lanka.

Sigiriya was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1982. It is recognised as one of the best-preserved examples of ancient urban planning in South Asia. The site attracts over half a million tourists annually and is Sri Lanka's most-visited monument.

After King Kassapa died in battle against his brother Moggallana in 495 AD, Sigiriya was handed to Buddhist monks. It functioned as a monastery for over a thousand years before being gradually abandoned. British archaeologist H.C.P. Bell rediscovered and began excavating it in the late 19th century.

The Boulder Gardens of Sigiriya consist of large natural and modified boulders arranged with caves, pathways, and cisterns carved into them. Buddhist monks used these caves as meditation retreats. Drip ledges carved above the cave entrances kept rainwater from dripping inside.
Sigiriya is located near the town of Dambulla in central Sri Lanka, within the Cultural Triangle of Sri Lanka, which also includes Anuradhapura, Polonnaruwa, and the Dambulla Cave Temple.

The height of Sigiriya rock is approximately 200 metres or 660 feet above the surrounding plains. The summit covers about 1.6 hectares. The total extent of the fortified area including the outer moat and gardens is about 80 hectares.

King Kassapa I reigned from 477 to 495 AD. He built Sigiriya to serve as both his royal palace and military fortress. His choice to build on the rock may also have had religious significance, as the rock resembles the mythical Mount Meru of Buddhist cosmology.

The frescoes at Sigiriya were created using a technique of applying lime plaster over the rock face, then painting while the plaster was still fresh. The colours used were yellow ochre, red, green, and black, derived from minerals found locally.
